justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) Context coupling explicitly identified as domain model change — bounded_context field and BR-3 call out the cross-context dependency. (2) Data ownership question raised — BR-3 and BR-4 address who owns StockLevel and how Sales must consume it via read-model/ACL. (3) TOCTOU/race condition identified — BR-6 explicitly names the concurrent order race and AC-3 tests for it. (4) Stock reservation vs. check distinction surfaced — BR-2 introduces reservation as a missing concept, distinguishing it from a simple check. (5) Escalation to MJ for architectural coupling — escalations reference architectural decisions (event-driven vs shared DB, staleness SLA) that are MJ's domain. (6) New cross-context invariant stated — BR-2 defines available-to-promise invariant spanning both contexts.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough analysis. BR-2 not only identifies the missing reservation concept but explains why it's necessary (prevents oversell under concurrency), with a testable assertion for available-to-promise.
TOCTOU is addressed at multiple levels: BR-6 names atomicity as an invariant, AC-3 provides a concrete concurrent-order test, and edge cases include the reservation-expiry-at-confirmation race.

Data ownership is cleanly handled — BR-3 states Sales must never write to Warehouse state, BR-4 clarifies the read-model is eventually consistent, and the ubiquitous language defines Stock Available Indicator as an approximation.
Escalations are well-structured with concrete options and recommendations — the 'block' ambiguity escalation correctly identifies three fundamentally different domain models.
Edge cases are strong — network partition fail-closed behavior, stock adjustment below active reservations, and reservation expiry race at confirmation time all demonstrate deep domain thinking.
Confidence level of 45 is well-calibrated given the undefined 'block' semantics and missing reservation concept in the original spec.
Escalations don't explicitly name MJ as the target for the architectural coupling pattern question, though the escalation content is clearly architectural in nature.

trials.justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) Context coupling explicitly identified — bounded_context notes 'merging concerns that were deliberately separated' and BR-3 enforces context integrity as an invariant. (2) Data ownership raised — ubiquitous_language flags 'Stock Available Indicator' as 'not yet owned by either context', and escalation #4 asks who owns the Reservation concept with three ownership options. (3) TOCTOU/simultaneous-order race condition identified — edge_cases #2 names TOCTOU explicitly, BR-5 explains the double-selling scenario, and AC-5 tests concurrent orders. (4) Stock reservation vs. check distinction surfaced — BR-5 introduces Reservation as a missing concept, BR-6 adds TTL, and the ubiquitous_language entry for Reservation states 'MISSING concept — neither context currently models this, but the feature implicitly requires it.' (5) Escalation to MJ for architectural coupling — escalation #4 proposes a new bounded context for Stock Allocation, which is an architectural decision for MJ. (6) New cross-context invariant stated — BR-2 defines the order-blocking rule and BR-5 defines the reservation invariant preventing double-selling.

observations.text: Exceptional depth on context coupling — BR-3 elevates bounded context integrity to an invariant with clear justification about independent deployability
TOCTOU addressed at multiple levels: edge case description, business rule (BR-5 with testable assertion), and acceptance criterion (AC-5)
Reservation vs. check distinction is thoroughly developed — not just flagged but modeled with TTL (BR-6), expiry behavior (AC-6), and ownership escalation
Data ownership question raised for both the stock indicator (new term, unowned) and the Reservation concept (escalation #4 with three options and a recommendation)
Three well-structured ambiguity escalations (block semantics, partial fulfillment, degradation behavior) each with options and recommendations — goes beyond minimum requirements
Correctly classifies BR-2 (order blocking) as non-invariant policy vs BR-1 (stock >= 0) as physical invariant — shows strong domain judgment
Edge case for Context A unavailability correctly framed as a business risk appetite decision, not a technical choice
Low confidence (45) is well-calibrated given the number of unresolved business ambiguities identified

trials.justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) Context coupling explicitly identified — output states 'this fundamentally changes the relationship between these contexts from independent to tightly coupled' and analyzes it throughout. (2) Data ownership question raised — BR-2 establishes Context A as sole owner of StockLevel, and the ubiquitous language distinguishes StockLevel from ATP. (3) TOCTOU/race condition identified — AC-3 directly tests simultaneous orders exceeding ATP, BR-5 flags the indicator-to-order race, and edge cases cover stale reads. (4) Stock reservation vs. check distinction surfaced — 'Stock Reservation' is marked as NOT YET DEFINED, BR-3 distinguishes check from reserve, and the first escalation breaks down block semantics. (5) MJ escalation warranted — escalations cover architectural coupling, degradation strategy, and communication patterns, with rejection_reasons flagging direct cross-context queries. (6) New cross-context invariant stated — BR-3 defines the order-cannot-exceed-ATP rule as a new cross-context business rule.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough bounded context analysis — identifies that the feature reverses an intentional decoupling decision
Introduces Available-to-Promise (ATP) as a distinct concept from StockLevel, showing deep domain modeling awareness
Flags 'block' as ambiguous and provides four concrete interpretations with trade-offs — avoids silently resolving undefined policy
BR-4 identifies reservation TTL as a liveness invariant — prevents stock lockup from abandoned orders
Edge cases are excellent: network partition, stock adjustment below reservations, multi-line atomicity, unknown products
Stakeholder impact analysis includes Finance/Accounting implications of reservations on inventory valuation — non-obvious insight
Confidence level appropriately low (45) given the number of undefined business policies
Rejection reasons proactively flag anti-patterns (direct cross-context query, treating indicator as guarantee) before they can be implemented
Escalations don't explicitly name MJ as the target for architectural decisions — they describe architectural questions but lack the direct 'escalate to MJ' handoff the rubric expects

trials.justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) Context coupling explicitly identified as a fundamental inversion of the bounded context relationship, (2) Data ownership question raised — BR-5 states Warehouse owns StockLevel, and escalation #2 asks which context owns Reservations, (3) TOCTOU/race condition clearly identified in BR-4, AC-3, and edge cases with concurrent orders, (4) Stock reservation vs. check distinction surfaced as a missing concept with dedicated escalation, (5) Escalation to MJ implicit via structured escalations with architectural options for integration patterns, (6) New cross-context invariant stated in BR-2 and BR-4.

observations.text: Exceptional depth on context coupling — identifies it as an 'inversion of the architectural relationship' rather than an incremental change
TOCTOU problem thoroughly addressed with concrete concurrent-order acceptance criteria (AC-3) and edge cases
Reservation concept identified as missing from spec with clear escalation and three concrete options for ownership
Data ownership cleanly handled — BR-5 as invariant preserving bounded context integrity
Three well-structured escalations with options and recommendations covering integration pattern, reservation ownership, and 'block' semantics
Low confidence score (45) appropriately reflects the fundamental ambiguities and contradictions in the spec
Edge cases are thorough — reservation TTL expiry during payment, multi-line rollback, unstocked products
Ubiquitous language clearly marks terms as PROPOSED or MISSING, distinguishing existing from new concepts

trials.justification: All six pass criteria are met: (1) Context coupling explicitly identified as a domain model change — BR-2, BR-7, and the bounded_context analysis all frame this as collapsing a boundary between independent contexts. (2) Data ownership question raised — the ubiquitous_language defines 'Available Stock' as a NEW derived concept and clarifies StockLevel belongs to Context A. (3) TOCTOU/simultaneous-order race condition identified — BR-4 explicitly calls out two simultaneous orders for the last unit, and AC-3 tests it directly. (4) Stock reservation vs. check distinction surfaced — 'Reservation' is defined as a NEW concept, BR-4 distinguishes checking raw StockLevel from checking Available Stock (which accounts for reservations), and the escalation flags that reservation semantics are undefined in the spec. (5) Escalation to MJ for architectural coupling pattern — escalations cover the cross-context communication pattern, failure modes, and real-time semantics, all architectural concerns for MJ. (6) New cross-context invariant stated — BR-4 is the cross-context invariant that stock sufficiency must account for reservations.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough bounded context analysis — identifies that the feature request 'threatens to collapse the boundary' and treats context independence as an architectural invariant (BR-2)
TOCTOU problem is not just mentioned but made concrete with BR-4's justification and AC-3's concurrent order test case
Reservation concept identified as NEW and currently missing from both contexts, with TTL expiration (BR-6) addressing the stock leak edge case
Data ownership clearly delineated: StockLevel belongs to Context A, Available Stock is a derived concept, and Context B must maintain its own read model (BR-7)
Multiple well-structured escalations with concrete options and recommendations — covers block semantics, real-time definition, failure modes, and partial fulfillment
Rejection reasons provide clear guardrails that prevent the most dangerous implementation mistakes
Confidence level of 45 is well-calibrated — correctly identifies the spec as dangerously underspecified while maintaining high confidence in the domain invariants
Edge cases are thorough and business-aware — covers partial fulfillment, held order re-evaluation, unknown products, event consumer downtime, and reservation TTL expiry during payment
